reasoning:
1. **2026-01-11 (Jan 11) - 98%**: FIDE releases its rating list once a month on the 1st. The Wikipedia page as of early January 2026 already shows Firouzja ranked #7 with a rating of 2759. Since there are no mid-month official updates to this list, his January ranking will remain at #7 identically through January 11. A rank of 7 is "as high or higher" (i.e. rank <= 7) than his rank on Jan 4.

2. **2026-02-03 (Feb 3) - 90%**: Standard FIDE ratings for February are published on Feb 1. The only major classical super-tournament during this period is the Tata Steel Masters (Jan 16 - Feb 1). Because FIDE regulations stipulate that tournaments must conclude at least a few days before the end of the month to be rated in the immediately following list, Tata Steel 2026 will very likely be deferred to the March 2026 rating list. Consequently, the top 10 classical ratings will likely remain static between January and February, maintaining Firouzja's rank at 7.

3. **2026-04-04 (April 4) - 42%**: By April, the FIDE list will definitely reflect the results of Tata Steel and potentially early Spring events (e.g. Prague Chess Festival). Firouzja lies on an asymmetrical "cliff": while he only has one player highly vulnerable directly above him (Anish Giri at 2760, +1 point ahead), there is a crowded pack of intensely active, improving young stars tightly clustered just below him (Praggnanandhaa at 2758, Gukesh at 2754). With these competitors playing multiple super-tournaments, the mathematical probability that at least one overtakes him (pushing him down to #8) without him simultaneously passing Giri is significantly higher than a coin flip, making it more likely than not that his rank will end up >7 by April.
